steps.title: Identify what the claim requires
steps.content: The passage says Vanya's opinion has changed significantly. The correct quotation must show both what he believed before and what he believes now — a clear before-and-after contrast.

steps.title: Eliminate and match
steps.content:
• Choice A — Vanya says to Professor Serebrakoff, "For twenty-five years I have managed [the country estate], and have sent you the returns from it like the most honest of servants, and you have never given me one single word of thanks for my work, not one- neither in my youth nor now.": Shows current resentment about fair treatment, but no prior positive view to contrast with. No change evident. ✗
  • Choice B — Professor Serebrakoff says to Helena, "It is funny that everybody listens to [Vanya] and his old idiot of a mother, but the moment I open my lips you all begin to feel ill-treated.": This is the Professor's perspective, not Vanya's opinion. Wrong speaker entirely. ✗
  • Choice C — Vanya says to Professor Serebrakoff, "This place [the country estate] could never have been bought had I not renounced my inheritance in favor of my sister [the professor's late wife], whom I deeply loved-and what is more, I worked for ten years like an ox, and paid off the debt.": Shows Vanya's grievance about sacrifice, but no former admiration to contrast with. Still no change. ✗
  • Choice D — Vanya says to himself, "I was proud of [Professor Serebrakoff] and of his learning; I received all his words and writings as inspired and now? Now he has retired, and what is the total of his life? A blank! He is absolutely unknown, and his fame has burst like a soap-bubble. I have been deceived; I see that now, basely deceived.": Explicitly states what Vanya felt before (proud, admiring) and what he feels now (deceived, disillusioned). The before-and-after contrast is direct and complete. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
